message.abstract:
<jats:title>Summary</jats:title>
                <jats:p>Regional connectivity and land-based travel have been identified as important drivers of SARS-CoV-2 transmission. However, the generalizability of this finding is understudied outside of well-sampled, highly connected regions such as Europe. In this study, we investigated the relative contributions of regional and intercontinental connectivity to the source-sink dynamics of SARS-CoV-2 for Jordan and the wider Middle East. By integrating genomic, epidemiological and travel data we show that the source of introductions into Jordan was dynamic across 2020, shifting from intercontinental seeding from Europe in the early pandemic to more regional seeding for the period travel restrictions were in place. We show that land-based travel, particularly freight transport, drove introduction risk during the period of travel restrictions. Consistently, high regional connectivity and land-based travel also disproportionately drove Jordan’s export risk to other Middle Eastern countries. Our findings emphasize regional connectedness and land-based travel as drivers of viral transmission in the Middle East. This demonstrates that strategies aiming to stop or slow the spread of viral introductions (including new variants) with travel restrictions need to prioritize risk from land-based travel alongside intercontinental air travel to be effective.</jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Highlights</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="order">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Regional connectivity drove SARS-CoV-2 introduction risk in Jordan during the period travel restrictions were in place in genomic and travel data.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Land-based travel rather than air travel disproportionately drove introduction risk during travel restrictions.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>High regional connectivity disproportionately drove Jordan’s export risk, with significant contribution from land-based travel.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Regional transmission dynamics were underestimated in genomic data due to unrepresentative sampling.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Regional connectivity drove bidirectional transmission of SARS-CoV-2 in the Middle East during travel restrictions
